name: Document Title Intent
summary: UX vocabulary for the browser/page title — the text shown in the tab, window, history, and bookmarks (document.title / the <title> element). Declares where the base title comes from (static / route-derived / dynamic), how a site-level template wraps it (e.g. `%s | Acme`, or an absolute override / default fallback), and whether a title change is announced to assistive tech on client-side navigation. The title is treated as a composed, layered string — the base is owned here, while ambient decorations (the unread count from Notification Marker, an unsaved-changes dot, an environment tag) and transient overrides ('Saving…') are layered on without clobbering it. Routing-driven: in a single-page app the base title resolves per route on the navigation lifecycle.

source.description: Where the base title text comes from. `static` is a fixed title set once; `route` derives it from the active route (the common SPA case — each view declares its title); `dynamic` computes it from application state (e.g. the open document's name, a live score).

template.description: How a site-level template wraps the per-view base title. Mirrors the Next.js metadata title model. `template` wraps the base in a pattern (e.g. `%s | Acme` → `Inbox | Acme`); `absolute` declares the base IS the complete title and the template is skipped (for a landing/marketing page); `default` is the fallback title used when a view declares no base of its own.

announce.description: Whether a base-title change is announced to assistive technology on a client-side navigation. A full page load reads the new <title> automatically, but a SPA route change does not — so `live` announces the new title (via a polite live region) as part of the view-change contract; `off` suppresses it (e.g. for an in-place filter that changes the title but is not a navigation). Composes the Navigation intent's autofocus-on-activation contract — focus landing and title announcement are the two halves of making an SPA view change perceivable.

description:
The Document Title Intent is the UX vocabulary for the browser/page title — the text the user sees in the tab, the window chrome, the history entry, and a saved bookmark. It maps to the platform document.title property and the <title> element, which stay in sync. The title is not a single string one component owns; it is a composed, layered value, and this intent declares how each view contributes its share.

Source

  • static: A fixed title set once (a single-page tool, a marketing page).
  • route: Derived from the active route — each view declares its title and the title resolves on navigation. The common SPA case.
  • dynamic: Computed from application state — the open document's name, an unread count baked into the base, a live status.

Template

A site-level wrapper applied around the per-view base, following the widely-adopted Next.js metadata title model:

  • template: Wrap the base in a pattern — %s | Acme turns a base of Inbox into Inbox | Acme.
  • absolute: The base IS the full title; skip the template (a landing page that wants just Acme — the everything app, no suffix).
  • default: The fallback title shown when a view declares no base of its own.

Announce

  • live: Announce the new base title to assistive technology on a client-side navigation. A fresh page load reads <title> automatically; a SPA route change does not, so the title is announced via a polite live region. This is the audible half of a perceivable view change.
  • off: Do not announce — correct when the title changes but no navigation occurred (an in-place filter or sort that reflects state in the title).

Layering (composed, never clobbered)

Several concerns want to influence the title at once. This intent owns the base layer only; the others are layered on top in a fixed order so no contributor overwrites another. From innermost to outermost:

  • base: the page/route/static title — owned here.
  • template: the site-level wrapper (above).
  • decoration: ambient prefixes/suffixes from app state — the unread (3) from Notification Marker's title surface, an unsaved-changes •, a [DEV] environment tag.
  • transient: a temporary override that auto-restores — Saving…, Reconnecting….

The mechanism that resolves these layers into the single document.title write is the CustomTitleRegistry plug (the contract each layer satisfies is CustomTitleContributor) — those are implementation contracts, named here only so the composition boundary is clear.

Composition (cross-reference, do not duplicate)

  • Navigation: in an SPA the base title resolves on the same view-change lifecycle. The announce dimension pairs with Navigation's autofocus-on-activation contract — focus landing + title announcement together make a view change perceivable. Owned by Web Routing.
  • Notification Marker: its title surface (the (3) Inbox count prefix) is a decoration layer here, not a competing writer of document.title — it contributes a prefix and the registry composes it over the base.
